chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Welcome to the fourth lecture of 6.006.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Today, we are going to be talking about hashing.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Last lecture on Tuesday, Professor Solomon

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: was talking about set data structures, storing

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: things so that you can query items by their key,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: by what they intrinsically are, versus what Professor Demaine

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: was talking about last week, which was sequence data

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: structures, where we impose an external order on these items,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and we want you to maintain those.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But I'm not supporting operations

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: where I'm looking stuff up based on what they are.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That's what the set interface is for.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So we're going to be talking a little bit more

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: about the set interface today.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So last, on Tuesday, you saw two ways

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: of implementing the set interface, one

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: using just an unsorted array.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Just I threw these things in an array,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and I could do a linear scan of my items

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to support basically any of these operations.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It's a little exercise you can go through.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I think they show it to you in the recitation notes.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But if you'd like to implement it for yourself, that's fine.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And then we saw a slightly better data structure,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: at least for the find operations.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Can I look something up, whether this key is in my set interface?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We can do that faster.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We can do that in log n time with a build overhead that's

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: about n log n, because we showed you three ways to sort.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Two of them were n squared.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: One of them was n log n, which is as good as we showed you

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: how to do yesterday.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So the question then becomes, can I build that data

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: structure faster?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That'll be a subject of next week's Thursday lecture.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But this week, we're going to concentrate on this static

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: find.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We got log n, which is an exponential improvement

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: over linear.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But the question now becomes, can I

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: do faster than log n time?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And what we're going to do at the first part of this lecture

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is show you that no.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: What's up?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: No?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: OK.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That you can't do faster than log n time in the caveat

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that we are in a slightly more restricted model of computation

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that we were then what we introduced to you

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: a couple of weeks ago.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And then show, if we're not in that more constrained

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: model of computation, we can actually do faster.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And doing faster is, I mean, log n's already pretty good.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Log n is not going to be larger than 30 for any problem

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that you're going to be talking about in the real world

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: on real computers.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But a factor of 30 is still bad.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I would prefer to do faster with those constant factors

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: when I can.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It's not a constant factor.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It's a logarithmic factor.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But you get what I'm saying.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So what we're going to do is first prove

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that you can't do faster for find.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Does everyone understand or remember

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: what find key meant?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I have a key.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I have a bunch of items that have keys associated with them.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And I want to see if one of the items that I'm storing

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: contains a key that is the same as the one that I searched for.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: The item might contain other things.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But in particular, it has a search key

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that I'm maintaining the set on so

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that it supports find operations, search operations

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: based on that key quickly.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Does that make sense?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So there's the find one that we want to improve.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And we also want to improve this insert, delete.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We want to make this data structure dynamic,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: because we might do those operations quite a bit.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And so this lecture is about optimizing those three things.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So first, I'm going to show you that we

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: can't do faster than log n for find, which is a little weird.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: The model of computation I'm going

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to be proving this lower bound on,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'm saying that how I'm going to approach this

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is I'm going to say that any way that I store these,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: the items that I'm storing in this data structure,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: for any way I store these things,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: any algorithm of this certain type

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is going to require at least logarithmic time.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That's what we're going to try to prove.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And the model of computation that's

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: weaker than what we've been talking about previously

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is what I'm going to call the comparison model.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And a comparison model means is that the items, the objects

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'm storing, I can kind of think of them as black boxes.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I don't get to touch these things,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: except the only way that I can distinguish between them

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is to say, given a key and an item or two items,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I can do a comparison on those keys.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Are these keys the same?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Is this key bigger than this one?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Is it smaller than this one?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Those are kind of the only operations

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I get to do with them.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I don't get to look at what the, say, if the keys are numbers,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I don't get to look at what number that is.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I just get to take two keys and compare them.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And actually, all of the search algorithms

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that we saw on Tuesday were comparison sort algorithms.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: What you did was you stepped through the program.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: At some point, you came to a branch,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and you looked at two keys, and you branched based

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: on whether one key was bigger than another.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That was a comparison.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And then you moved some stuff around,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: but that was the general paradigm.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Those three sorting operations lived in this comparison model.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: You've got comparison operations like,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: are they equal, less than, greater than, maybe

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: greater than or equal, less than or equal.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Generally, you have all these operations that you could do,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: maybe not equal.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But the key thing here is that there are only

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: two possible outputs to each of these comparators.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: There's only two things.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: There's only one thing that I can branch on.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It's going to branch into two different lines.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It's either true, and I do some other computation,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: or it's false, and I'll do a different set of computation.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That make sense?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So what I'm going to do is I'm going to view a comparison,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: an algorithm in the comparison model,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: as what I like to call a decision tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So if I specify an algorithm to you,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: the first thing it's going to do,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: if I don't compare items at all, I'm

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: kind of screwed, because I'll never be able to tell

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: if my key's in there or not.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So I have to do some comparisons.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So I'll do some computation.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Maybe I find out the length of the array,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and I do some constant-time stuff.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But at some point, I'll do a comparison, and I'll branch.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'll come to this node, and if the comparison,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: maybe a less than, if it's true, I'm

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: going to go this way in my computation.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And if it's false, I'm going to go this way in my computation.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And I'm going to keep doing that with various comparisons,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: sure, until I get down here to some leaf in which I'm not

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: branching.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: The internal nodes here are representing comparisons,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: but the leaves are representing I stopped my computation.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'm outputting something.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Does that make sense, what I'm kind of trying to do?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'm kind of changing my algorithm

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to be put in this kind of graphical way

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: where I'm branching what my program could possibly

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: do based on the comparisons that I do.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'm not actually counting the rest of the work

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that the program does.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'm really only looking at the comparisons

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: because I know that I'll need to compare some things eventually

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to figure out what my items are.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And if that's the only way I can distinguish items,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: then I have to do those comparisons to find out.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Does that make sense?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So what I have is a binary tree that's

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: representing the comparisons done by my algorithm.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So it starts at one comparison, and then it branches.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: How many leaves must I have in my tree?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: What does that question mean in terms of the program?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: AUDIENCE 2.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: What's the number of comparisons?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: JASON KU.: What's that?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: AUDIENCE 2.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: The number of comparisons.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: No, that's the number of internal nodes

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that I have in the algorithm.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And actually, the number of comparisons

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that I do in an execution of the algorithm

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is just along a path from here to a leaf.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So what do the leaves actually represent?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Those that represent outputs.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'm going to output something here.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And yeah?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: AUDIENCE 3.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: The number of items.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: JASON KU.: The number of items.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So I need at least.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So what is the output to my search algorithm?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Maybe it's an index of an item that contains this key,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: or maybe I return the item is the output, the item

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: of the thing I'm storing.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And I'm storing n things, so I need at least n outputs,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: because I need to be able to return any of the items

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that I'm storing based on a different search key parameter

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: if it's going to be correct.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I actually need one more output.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Why do I need one more output?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If it's not in there.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So any correct comparison searching algorithm,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: where I'm doing some comparisons to find this thing,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: needs to have at least n plus 1 leaves.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Otherwise, it can't be correct, because I

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: could look up the one that I'm not returning in that set,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and it would never be able to return that value.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Does that make sense?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Yeah?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: What's n?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: n is always the number for a data structure.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: n is the number of things stored in that data

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: structure at that time, so the number of items

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in the data structure.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That's what it means in all of these tables.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Any other questions?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: OK, so now we get to the fun part.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: How many comparisons does this algorithm have to do?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Yeah, up there.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: What's up?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: All right, your colleague is jumping ahead for a second.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But really, I have to do as many comparisons, in the worst case,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: as the longest root-to-leaf path in this tree,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: because as I'm executing this algorithm,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'll go down this thing, always branching down.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And at some point, I'll get to a leaf.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And in the worst case, if I happen

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to need to return this particular output,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: then I'll have to walk down the longest thing.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It's the longest path.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And the longest path is the same as the height of the tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So the question then becomes, what

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is the minimum height of any binary tree that

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: has at least n plus 1 leaves?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Understand why we're asking that question?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: OK, so in restaurant, yeah?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: AUDIENCE 2.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Could you go over again why it needs an n plus 1 leaf?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: JASON KU.: Why it needs n plus 1 leaves?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If it's a correct algorithm, it needs

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to return, it needs to be able to return any of the n items

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that I'm storing, or say that the key that I'm looking for

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is not there.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Great question.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: OK, so what is the minimum height

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: of any binary tree that has n plus 1, at least n plus 1

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: leaves?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: You can solve that.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: You can actually state a recurrence for that

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and solve that.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: You're going to do that in your recitation.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But it's log n.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: The best you can do is if this is a balanced binary tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So the min height is going to be at least log n height.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Or the min height is logarithmic,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: so it's actually theta right here.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But if I just said height here, I

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: would be lower bounding the height.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I could have a linear height.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If I just chained comparisons down one by one,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: if I was doing a linear search, for example.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: All right, so this is saying that if I'm just

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: restricting to comparisons, I have

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to spend at least logarithmic time

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to be able to find whether this key is in my set.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But I don't want logarithmic time.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I want faster.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So how can I do that?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I have one operation in my model of computation

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I presented a couple of weeks ago

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that allows me to do faster, which

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: allows me to do something stronger than comparisons.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Comparisons have a constant branching factor.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: In particular, if I do this operation,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: this constant time operation, I can branch

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to two different locations.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It's like an if kind of situation, if or else.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And in fact, if I had constant branching

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: factor for any constant here, if I had 3 or 4,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: if it was bounded by a constant, the height of this tree

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: would still be bounded by a log base

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: of the constant of that number of leaves.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So I need, in some sense, to be able to branch

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: a non-constant amount.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So how can I branch a non-constant amount?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: This is a little tricky.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We had this really neat operation

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in the random access machine that we could randomly

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: go to any place in memory in constant time

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: based on a number.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That was a super powerful thing, because

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in a single constant time operation,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I could go to any space in memory.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That's potentially much larger than linear branching factor,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: depending on the size of my model

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and the size of my machine.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So that's a very powerful operation.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Can we use that to find quicker?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Anyone have any ideas?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Sure.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We're going to get to hashing in a second,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: but this is a simpler concept than hashing, something

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: you probably are familiar with already.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We've kind of been using it implicitly

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in some of our sequence data structure things.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: What we're going to do is, if I have an item that has key 10,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'm going to keep an array and store that item 10 spaces away

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: from the front of the array, at index 9 or the 10th index.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Does that make sense?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If I store that item at that location in memory,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I can use this random access to that location

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and see if there's something there.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If there's something there, I return that item.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Does that make sense?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: This is what I call a direct access array.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It's really no different than the arrays

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that we've been talking about earlier in the class.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We've got an array.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And if I have an item here with key equals 10,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'll stick it here in the 10th place.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Now, I can only now store one item with the key 10

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in my thing.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And that's one of the stipulations

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.text: I'm just going to do a linear find.

chunks.text: I could put any data structure here,
